name: Francisco Abellán Reservoir and ascent to Puerto los Blancares — from La Peza
name_original: Embalse de Francisco Abellán y subida a Puerto los Blancares — desde La Peza
region_intro: Road cycling around La Peza offers routes through varied terrain, characterized by reservoirs, mountain passes, and canyons. The region features ascents to elevated points like Puerto los Blancares, providing panoramic views. Cyclists can expect a landscape that includes both open valleys and more rugged sections, with routes often incorporating significant elevation changes. This area provides a diverse environment for road cycling, from scenic loops around water bodies to challenging climbs. Best road cycling routes around La Peza The most popular road cycling route is Francisco Abellán Reservoir and ascent to Puerto los Blancares — from La Peza, a 27.5 miles (44.2 km) trail that takes 2 hours 24 minutes to complete. This route features views of the reservoir and a notable climb to a mountain pass. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Quéntar Reservoir – Hoya de la Mora loop from Diezma, a difficult 103.1 miles (166.0 km) path. This extensive loop traverses reservoir areas and valleys, offering a demanding ride through diverse scenery. Local road cyclists also love the Mirador de la Magdalena – Cave Quarter, Guadix loop from Diezma, a 43.5 miles (70.0 km) trail leading through areas with scenic viewpoints and unique geological formations, often completed in about 3 hours 17 minutes. Road cycling around La Peza is defined by reservoirs, mountain ascents, and canyons. The network offers options for moderate to challenging abilities, with routes featuring significant elevation gains. The routes in La Peza are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 1,100 road cyclists have used komoot to explore La Peza's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around La Peza?
A: There are over 10 road cycling routes in the La Peza area, offering a range of experiences from moderate to challenging. The region is known for its varied terrain, including reservoirs, mountain passes, and canyons.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in La Peza?
A: While most routes around La Peza are moderate to difficult, the region's terrain is generally characterized by significant elevation changes. There are no routes explicitly categorized as easy in the current selection, but moderate routes can be approached with caution by less experienced cyclists. Always check the route profile before you go.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around La Peza?
A: Road cycling around La Peza features diverse terrain, including scenic loops around reservoirs, challenging ascents to mountain passes like Puerto los Blancares, and rides through canyons. You'll encounter both open valleys and more rugged sections, often with notable elevation gains.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Yes, La Peza offers challenging routes for experienced cyclists. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39563805" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Quéntar Reservoir – Hoya de la Mora loop from Diezma&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 166.0 km path with significant elevation, traversing reservoir areas and valleys for a demanding ride.

Q: What are some scenic viewpoints or attractions along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer scenic views. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5289612" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador de la Magdalena – Cave Quarter, Guadix loop from Diezma&lt;/a&gt; leads through areas with scenic viewpoints and unique geological formations. You might also encounter highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3525967" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Quéntar Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6359134" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;End of the World Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any loop routes available for road cycling?
A: Yes, many road cycling routes around La Peza are designed as loops. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39563805" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Quéntar Reservoir – Hoya de la Mora loop from Diezma&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/5289612" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirador de la Magdalena – Cave Quarter, Guadix loop from Diezma&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31560401" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Belerda Canyon loop from Los Villares&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best season for road biking in La Peza?
A: The region's climate, characterized by its inland location, generally offers pleasant conditions for cycling during spring and autumn. Summers can be hot, especially at lower elevations, while winters might bring cooler temperatures to the mountain passes. Always check local weather forecasts before planning your ride.

Q: What do other cyclists say about road cycling in La Peza?
A: The road cycling routes in La Peza are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 10 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the challenging ascents, and the scenic beauty of the reservoirs and canyons.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by the Belerda Canyon?
A: Yes, the Belerda Canyon is a notable feature in the area. You can explore it on routes such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31560326" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Belerda Canyon – Follapava loop from Diezma&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31560401" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Belerda Canyon loop from Los Villares&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the typical duration for road cycling routes in this area?
A: Route durations vary significantly based on distance and elevation. For example, a moderate route like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983642813/francisco-abellan-reservoir-and-ascent-to-puerto-los-blancares-from-la-peza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Francisco Abellán Reservoir and ascent to Puerto los Blancares — from La Peza&lt;/a&gt; (44.2 km) takes approximately 2 hours 24 minutes, while a more extensive and difficult route can take much longer.

Q: Are there any routes that include a climb to Puerto los Blancares?
A: Yes, the ascent to Puerto los Blancares is a prominent feature in the region. The route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983642813/francisco-abellan-reservoir-and-ascent-to-puerto-los-blancares-from-la-peza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Francisco Abellán Reservoir and ascent to Puerto los Blancares — from La Peza&lt;/a&gt; specifically includes this notable climb, offering panoramic views from the pass.
tips: Located near the Spanish town of Lopera, on the bed of the Fardes River, the reservoir is basically used for agricultural irrigation. Its construction meant a great change to the situation of uncertainty due to the dry seasons in the region occupied by the Vega de la Hoya de Guadix.
Road from Blancares to La Peza. Perfect place to shoot, good asphalt, little traffic and a spectacular setting

Puerto de los Blancares, 1297m Puerto de los Blancares is a mountain pass in the Sierra Nevada, Granada, at an altitude of 1297 metres. It is on the GR-3201 route, which connects La Peza with Granada. The route is approximately 9.3 kilometres long and offers scenic scenery with an average gradient of 3.4%. It is a popular route for walkers and cyclists due to the beautiful scenery and the challenging, but advantageous, gradient. The route starts near the Pantano de Quéntar and passes through forests and open terrain.
